# Week 2: Qiskit Programming

## Video 1

### Introduction to Qiskit

- [Music] hello everyone welcome to the week two of our course introduction to quantum computing quantum algorithms and kiskit this week we are going to focus on the different ways to program on an actual quantum computer i am anupama i am a research scientist at ibm research india i am also a quantum ambassador and a casket advocate in today's session we are going to learn about the two ways that ibm has created to program our actual ibm quantum computers the first way is a simple graphical user interface method wherein you can drag and drop gates and operators and create circuits see all the visualization right there and the second way is the pythonic way which is also called the quantum information science toolkit or kiskit and it's also open source so anyone who is more familiar with python can use kisket and people who are wanting to use quantum computers as a tool then to understand and and to you know make progress in their relevant fields can use the circuit composer as well a lot of the physicists chemistry chemistry folks uh people from life science background usually prefer the circuit composer and it's a very easy relatively new um not a huge learning curve and it's easy to understand and create your own circuits on circuit composer again with the pythonic version if someone knows a little bit of programming and they know a little bit of python then it's directly written on top of python so kisk doesn't need any other new language understanding or familiarity with another language if you know python you can get started on kiskit now today's session we are going to first focus on navigating through this dashboard called ibm quantum experience where you can find both circuit composer and kiskit in the same place and and on cloud so you can directly access you can see the hardwares the simulators that you have access to and you can also see all the documentations the tutorials the open source kit textbook and various other self learning reference material all listed in the same place so we're going to walk through navigate through this portal see all that it has to offer and then make use of couple of things and then we're going to dive into our circuit composer and quickly build some circuits the circuits that we have learned in week one involving the use of single qubit gate multiple qubit gate and creating the you know quantum phenomena such as superposition and entanglement so all you need to do to set up and actually get access to a ibm quantum computer is log on to the this portal called quantum hyphen computing.ibm.com if you have an ibm id you can use that and log in if you have a gmail id or a linkedin id or a facebook id or a twitter id you can use that and sign up in this page as well once you sign up on this page you will get access and you can see all the backend hardware um the simulators the composer the quantum lab or kiskit jupiter notebooks everything in the same place also once you log in on the on your screen on the top right corner you see your profile avatar you click on that and you go to account details you can actually see something called install kiskit where from there you can directly just click and install kiskit locally on your laptop that means you have the kisket version which has all the latest developments and you can also use the api token and directly fire from here as well and we're going to come up and show all the other ways of using cascade and programming with kiskat as well in this in the second half of the session so this is the kind of dashboard the dashboard is available in different um you know themes so if your system theme is white you can have it in white or if you prefer a dark theme you can do it in a dark theme as i particularly prefer when i am coding then you'll see on this on the right hand side of your screen uh you have all the news all the notifications

updated from here now coming to the main part of the screen here you see two buttons one is a launch composer and then is a launch quantum lab so launch composer is the circuit composer or the ui method of creating quantum circuits so we are going to do that and in the first half and the second half we're going to focus on the quantum lab which is the pythonic way or using it from directly from this dashboard on cloud and then you have a couple of other things so you have all the services available to you so you can just directly click on view oil and see what are the different hardwares different system services and the simulators that are available to you so you click on systems and then you can see what are the different hardwares available to you if you see here these are all the systems that are available at any given point in time you will see there is a different number of systems that are available since 2016 when ibm released its first quantum computer on cloud there have been multiple uh quantum computers that have been put on cloud and are openly available for people to use so if you click on your systems then you get these eight hardware so these are the eight hardwares that are openly available and all of you will have access to these there are mostly uh these five qubit machines and there is a one qubit machine as well and then if you go down to all the systems that are currently available so you see 20 systems currently available every day at least once in a day these systems are calibrated so at any given point in time um you know you have a maximum number of systems not all the 29 computers but a maximum number of systems so at this point of time we have 20 computers 20 quantum computers that are available and and you can see uh their status here that okay that's it's online so it's available what is the processor type you can go up and read about the processor type so you can just click here learn more and see okay what is what are each of the processor types and if you remember from your week one invited talk where you saw the hardware roadmap of ibm where it was mentioned that you know we have uh we came up with uh the canary processor in 2018 which is up to 16 cubic then in 2019 we had a major improvement we were able to move from 16 to 27 cubits with our falcon processor and then in 2020 we were able to move from 27 qubit to 65 qubit machine with our hummingbird processor in 2020 there was also a couple of other improvements over the falcon processor itself and if you see here you can see that the falcon processor with 27 cubits initially had a quantum volume of 32 which in the same in the next year 2020 was in doubled to quantum volume of 64 and then again quadruple to quantum volume of 128. now if you remember quantum volume quantum volume is a metric and it is it is uh performed it is a metric to measure the performance of actual quantum computers or what this performance of the quantum computers mean so quantum volume is a single uh is a single number metric and it is used to measure the performance of quantum computers in the in a sense that this particular number that you saw on your screen 128 64 or 32 is the largest random circuit of equal width and depth that this

particular computer can successfully implement so this particular parameter or this particular metric it does not take into account just the number of qubits but it also takes into an account a lot of these parameters and these parameters are very very useful parameters so if you just think of each of them so uh you know um the way with falcon processor in 2020 uh we were able to improve our uh quantum volume from 32 to 64 was mostly you know all thanks to the circuit optimizations that the team was able to build so circuit optimization refers to how well an optimizing compiler improves the layout and performance of the circuit across its depth and qubit register width so there is a lot of improvement and these are software improvements that were done to the system to pull the system from a quantum volume of 32 to a point of volume of 64. and there are of course several other parameters here as you can see uh parameters like coherence how long does the qubit stay useful uh parameters like calibration error and so this is like a hardware metric then of course the number of qubits the spectator errors so when a qubit is lying idle there you know uh what is the impact on the nearby qubits and of course the coupling map and so on and you can you can see more about uh the all these all these on on your dashboard itself and i'll show you uh from there also if you want to uh because a quantum volume is measured classically so if you want to see how to measure quantum volume the entire ipython notebook is present here in this uh in the jupyter notebook is present in the kisket textbook in the textbook is an open source book so you can directly go to this link and and yourself try in snippets and understand more about quantum volume now this is this quantum volume was a paper uh you know released in 2018 from ibm and it is now a widely accepted industry standard uh metric to be able to measure the performance of quantum computers so there are a couple of more things that you can do here so uh let if you click on the any of these hardwares uh so you can see all the details about this hardware as well from this dashboard so the first thing that you see here is you don't have access to this particular system this is a premier system with 65 qubits and there are ways to uh get access to premier systems and i'll put i'll give you pointers to all of that as well but this the system that you see is even after having 65 qubits has a quantum volume of 32 versus you saw falcon cube systems with 27 cubits reaching up to a quantum volume of 128 and the reason is that it is quantum volume is not just dependent on the number of qubits there was a study in university of tokyo where they where they actually showed that they didn't increase the number of qubits but if they could decrease the error 10 times then the overall improvement in quantum volume is 500 times so error is definitely one of the biggest one brackets to impact but there are several others as i showed you circuit optimization itself was able to quadruple in a year the overall quantum volume of the system or the overall performance of that particular quantum processor so ah here also there are other couple of information which is important and for different kinds of people different kinds of information might be important ah for example if you see the average c naught error so there are a couple of c naught gates and the c naught gate errors range from uh you know a certain point to a certain point and it will give you okay where is the average c naught error uh line for this particular machine what is the average t one time or t two time or the coherence and how long the qubits stay in this useful state or stay in the state of superposition because that is how long you can do your computations as well and what are the basis uh gates that are used to build this network you see the topology here like the way each of the

the reason simulators are are very important is that today the the hardware that is available today is still noisy so there are some noise in the results and the measurements that you finally are being able to do so this these are the simulators and you can using simulators you can actually do not just your cert not just creating your circuits and getting some outputs but also noise modeling and and then you can actually say that okay in a noisy environment uh which is modeled in this function ah this is how my circuit is going to behave so even if there is not a quantum computer uh to do that work you can still do within the simulators so there are these five simulators but uh for most part of the first session today we are going to use the ibm custom simulator custom stands for quantum assembly language and this is a 32 qubit machine it is it is a general context aware machine and it is capable of doing uh noise modeling as well it is uh used for both ideal simulation and noise modelling and the simulation algorithm is automatically selected here ah based on the kind of input circuit parameters that is that you provide now uh if you see an interesting thing and and i have always got a lot of questions about this that this is a 32 qubit system and there is a there is a 5 000 qubit system versus the real hardware you are still in 65 qubit but in a simulator you have reached up to 5000 cubits now this particular simulator uh note is it's a clifford simulators it's a very efficient simulator for clifford circuits and it can also simulate noise evolution if the noise operators are clifford gates similarly for the mps uh simulator so mps is a is it's a this is a tensor network simulator that uses matrix product state representation for the state and that is often uh very efficient for states with weak entanglement you can read about each of these simulators once you click on them and and then for now we will just go back and and just because we wanted to discuss about uh the you know the different ways to access these hardwares uh so i'll just focus one second on on the different ways of accessing these premier systems so if you are an academic person
